content.text: PagedAttention and continuous batching work as a system: paged KV cache eliminates memory fragmentation so more requests fit in GPU memory simultaneously, and continuous batching keeps the GPU busy with those requests every single decode step. The throughput improvement is multiplicative, not additive. Understanding
them is essential for capacity planning and cost optimization in production.

attrs.alt: Throughput vs concurrent requests: vLLM vs TGI vs HuggingFace, and latency breakdown

content.text: LLM serving has two distinct throughput metrics that are often conflated:
): total generated tokens per second across all concurrent requests. This is the primary GPU utilization metric — a higher number means the GPU's matrix multiply units are doing more useful work.
): completed requests per second. For equal-length outputs this is proportional to token throughput; for variable lengths it reflects the rate at which users get their answers.
Per-request latency breaks into two components:
: time from request arrival until the first generated token is returned. Dominated by prefill compute and queue wait time. Interactive applications are most sensitive to this.
: average time between successive tokens during decoding, equal to
. Real-time streaming requires TPOT < ~50ms to feel fluent.
The relationship between throughput and latency follows a

queuing trade-off: as you increase concurrency to maximize throughput, TTFT grows because new requests wait longer in queue. The operating point is set by your SLA — typically TTFT < 2–5s and TPOT < 20–50ms for chat applications.
Why PagedAttention Raises Throughput: The Memory–Batch Link
The direct mechanism is simple: more efficient memory use → larger effective batch size → higher GPU compute utilization → more output tokens per second. Quantitatively:
is the mean concurrent batch size,
is mean request completion time. Under static batching, fragmentation caps
— for a log-normal output distribution with median ~120 tokens vs. max_len=4096, that is a 30× larger batch capacity.

The GPU's tensor cores saturate at moderate batch sizes (typically B > 8–16 for a 7B model), so pushing B from 2 to 64 converts idle silicon into useful work rather than proportionally increasing compute cost. This is why throughput gains are super-linear at low batch sizes and flatten once tensor cores are fully utilized.
Measuring Throughput with vLLM's Benchmark Script
vLLM ships a dedicated throughput benchmark. Run it against a live vLLM server or in offline mode:

# Start vLLM OpenAI-compatible server
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
  --model meta-llama/Llama-3-8b-hf \
  --gpu-memory-utilization 0.85 \
  --max-num-seqs 256 &

# Benchmark throughput (offline mode, no separate server needed)
python benchmarks/benchmark_throughput.py \
  --backend vllm \
  --model meta-llama/Llama-3-8b-hf \
  --dataset-name random \
  --num-prompts 1000 \
  --input-len 512 \
  --output-len 256 \
  --gpu-memory-utilization 0.85

# Expected output (A100 80GB):
# Throughput: 2341.7 requests/min, 598,835.3 tokens/min
# Latency (mean): 0.32s, P99: 0.81s
For a head-to-head comparison across frameworks:

# Compare vLLM vs HuggingFace text-generation-inference
# All tests: Llama-3-8B, 1000 prompts, input=256 output=256 tokens

# vLLM
python benchmarks/benchmark_throughput.py \
  --backend vllm --model meta-llama/Llama-3-8b-hf \
  --num-prompts 1000 --input-len 256 --output-len 256

# HuggingFace Transformers (static batch of 8)
python benchmarks/benchmark_throughput.py \
  --backend hf --model meta-llama/Llama-3-8b-hf \
  --num-prompts 1000 --input-len 256 --output-len 256 \
  --hf-max-batch-size 8

# TGI (requires running TGI server separately)
python benchmarks/benchmark_throughput.py \
  --backend tgi --model meta-llama/Llama-3-8b-hf \
  --num-prompts 1000 --input-len 256 --output-len 256 \
  --endpoint http://localhost:8080
For workload-specific numbers that reflect your production request distribution:

"""
Production-representative throughput benchmark.
Simulates Poisson arrivals with realistic output-length distribution.
"""
import asyncio, time, statistics, numpy as np
from openai import AsyncOpenAI

client = AsyncOpenAI(base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1", api_key="x")

async def single_request(prompt: str, max_tokens: int) -> dict:
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    first_token_time = None
    tokens = 0

    async with client.chat.completions.with_streaming_response.create(
        model="meta-llama/Llama-3-8b-hf",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        max_tokens=max_tokens,
        stream=True,
    ) as resp:
        async for chunk in resp:
            if chunk.choices and chunk.choices[0].delta.content:
                if first_token_time is None:
                    first_token_time = time.perf_counter()
                tokens += 1

    total_time = time.perf_counter() - t0
    ttft = first_token_time - t0 if first_token_time else total_time
    tpot = (total_time - ttft) / max(tokens - 1, 1)
    return {"ttft": ttft, "tpot": tpot, "tokens": tokens, "total": total_time}


async def benchmark(num_requests: int = 200, arrival_rate: float = 10.0):
    """
    arrival_rate: mean requests per second (Poisson).
    Lower rate = light load; higher = heavy load.
    """
    rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
    prompts = [f"Explain concept number {i} in detail." for i in range(num_requests)]
    # Log-normal output distribution
    max_tokens_list = rng.lognormal(4.8, 1.5, num_requests).clip(8, 512).astype(int).tolist()
    # Poisson inter-arrival times
    intervals = rng.exponential(1.0 / arrival_rate, num_requests).tolist()

    tasks = []
    t_start = time.perf_counter()
    for i, (prompt, max_tok, interval) in enumerate(zip(prompts, max_tokens_list, intervals)):
        await asyncio.sleep(interval)
        tasks.append(asyncio.create_task(single_request(prompt, max_tok)))

    results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t_start

    ttfts = [r["ttft"] for r in results]
    tpots = [r["tpot"] for r in results]
    total_tokens = sum(r["tokens"] for r in results)

    print(f"=== Benchmark Results (arrival_rate={arrival_rate:.1f} req/s) ===")
    print(f"Completed:       {len(results)} requests in {elapsed:.1f}s")
    print(f"Token throughput: {total_tokens/elapsed:.0f} tok/s")
    print(f"Request rate:     {len(results)/elapsed:.1f} req/s")
    print(f"TTFT p50={statistics.median(ttfts)*1000:.0f}ms  p99={sorted(ttfts)[int(len(ttfts)*0.99)]*1000:.0f}ms")
    print(f"TPOT p50={statistics.median(tpots)*1000:.0f}ms  p99={sorted(tpots)[int(len(tpots)*0.99)]*1000:.0f}ms")

# Run at different load levels to find the knee of the latency curve
for rate in [1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 20.0]:
    asyncio.run(benchmark(200, arrival_rate=rate))
These numbers are approximate and depend on exact batch size, request distribution, chunked prefill settings, and driver versions. Always benchmark on your specific workload.

For models larger than a single GPU's VRAM, tensor parallelism (TP) shards weight matrices across GPUs. Throughput scales near-linearly with TP degree for small models (TP=2→2×) but flattens for larger TP (TP=8→~6×) due to all-reduce communication overhead:

from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams
import time, os

MODEL = "meta-llama/Llama-3-70b-hf"  # requires 2+ A100s

for tp in [1, 2, 4]:
    if tp > int(os.environ.get("AVAILABLE_GPUS", 1)):
        print(f"TP={tp}: skipping (not enough GPUs)")
        continue

    llm = LLM(
        model=MODEL,
        tensor_parallel_size=tp,
        gpu_memory_utilization=0.85,
    )
    params = SamplingParams(max_tokens=256, temperature=0.0)
    prompts = ["Explain distributed systems." for _ in range(128)]

    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    outputs = llm.generate(prompts, params)
    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0

    total_tok = sum(len(o.outputs[0].token_ids) for o in outputs)
    print(f"TP={tp}: {total_tok/elapsed:.0f} tok/s")
    del llm

# Expected (4× A100 80GB, Llama-3-70B):
# TP=1: OOM (70B model doesn't fit in one 80GB A100)
# TP=2: ~420 tok/s
# TP=4: ~750 tok/s (~1.8x, not 2x, due to all-reduce)
Plotting TTFT against throughput as you sweep arrival rate gives the

— the Pareto frontier of achievable performance. Points on the curve are achievable; points below-left are leaving performance on the table; points above-right are infeasible. A production deployment should choose an operating point on the curve that satisfies TTFT SLA while maximizing throughput:

"""
Sweep arrival rate and record (throughput, TTFT_p99) to plot the operating curve.
"""
import asyncio, json
# Re-use the benchmark() function from the earlier code block
from typing import Awaitable

results_curve = []
for rate in [0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 8.0, 12.0, 18.0, 25.0]:
    stats = asyncio.run(benchmark(num_requests=300, arrival_rate=rate))
    results_curve.append({"rate": rate, **stats})

# Print markdown table
print("| Arrival rate | Throughput (tok/s) | TTFT p50 (ms) | TTFT p99 (ms) |")
print("|---|---|---|---|")
for r in results_curve:
    print(f"| {r['rate']} req/s | {r.get('throughput', '?'):.0f} | {r.get('ttft_p50', '?'):.0f} | {r.get('ttft_p99', '?'):.0f} |")
# The curve will show: throughput plateaus near GPU-max; TTFT grows sharply past the saturation point.
# The "knee" (highest throughput with acceptable TTFT) is your target operating point.
for 8B models on 80GB: below ~64, tensor cores are under-utilized at decode time.
prevents long prompts from blocking decoding steps.
: start at 0.85–0.90; lower if you see preemption warnings in logs (
Preempting request due to lack of KV cache blocks
metric. If it exceeds 0 at your target TTFT, scale horizontally.
for ~3× throughput multiplier with minimal accuracy loss on well-trained models.
Roofline Analysis: Where Is the Bottleneck?
Understanding whether your serving setup is
determines where optimization effort pays off. The roofline model plots attainable FLOP/s against arithmetic intensity (FLOPs per byte of HBM access):

is arithmetic intensity in FLOPs/byte. Decoding with batch size 1 has very low intensity (~1 FLOP/byte for weight loads) — it is deep in the memory-bandwidth bound region. As batch size grows, matrix-vector ops become matrix-matrix ops and intensity rises toward the compute roof. PagedAttention raises
without increasing latency per-token, pushing the operating point right along the x-axis toward higher intensity:

"""
Estimate arithmetic intensity for LLM decoding at various batch sizes.
Shows why larger batches (enabled by PagedAttention) improve GPU utilization.
"""

def decode_intensity(batch_size: int, d_model: int = 4096, num_layers: int = 32,
                     dtype_bytes: int = 2) -> dict:
    """
    Approximate FLOPs and bytes accessed per decode step for one batch.
    Simplified: considers only the FFN and attention linear layers.
    """
    # Per-layer: 4 weight matrices in attention (Q, K, V, O) + 2 in FFN (gate+up, down)
    # Each is (batch, d_model) @ (d_model, d_model) → 2*batch*d_model^2 FLOPs
    attn_flops = batch_size * 4 * 2 * d_model * d_model  # 4 projections
    ffn_flops = batch_size * 2 * 2 * d_model * (4 * d_model)  # 2 FFN layers
    total_flops = num_layers * (attn_flops + ffn_flops)

    # Bytes: weight matrices dominate (loaded fresh each step at small batch)
    attn_weight_bytes = 4 * d_model * d_model * dtype_bytes
    ffn_weight_bytes = 2 * (d_model * 4 * d_model) * dtype_bytes
    total_bytes = num_layers * (attn_weight_bytes + ffn_weight_bytes)
    # At large batch, weight load is amortized; per-token byte cost falls
    bytes_per_token = total_bytes / batch_size  # weights shared across batch

    intensity = total_flops / total_bytes  # FLOPs / total_bytes (not per-token)

    # A100 HBM bandwidth: 2 TB/s, peak TFLOPs (fp16): 312
    hbm_bw = 2e12  # bytes/s
    peak_tflops = 312e12

    attainable_tflops = min(peak_tflops, intensity * hbm_bw)
    utilization = attainable_tflops / peak_tflops

    return {
        "batch_size": batch_size,
        "intensity_flop_byte": intensity,
        "attainable_pct_peak": utilization * 100,
    }

print(f"{'Batch':>6}  {'Intensity (F/B)':>16}  {'% Peak TFLOPS':>14}")
for bs in [1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128]:
    r = decode_intensity(bs)
    print(f"{bs:>6}  {r['intensity_flop_byte']:>16.1f}  {r['attainable_pct_peak']:>14.1f}%")

# Output shows: batch=1 → ~0.3% of peak; batch=64 → ~19%; batch=256 → ~60%
# PagedAttention enables batch=64+ in the same memory that would hold batch=2 under contiguous allocation
Higher throughput directly reduces inference cost. If a cloud GPU instance costs $X per hour and delivers Y output tokens per second, the cost per million tokens is:
For example, an A100 80GB at $3.00/hr delivering 2400 tok/s costs:
The same hardware delivering only 600 tok/s (HF static batching) costs $1.39/MTok — 4× more. At scale, this gap is the primary economic argument for PagedAttention-based serving:
Key takeaway: vLLM on an A100 matches the cost efficiency of a naive setup on hardware twice as expensive. Switching to FP8 on H100 further halves the cost.
Saturating the GPU: When Throughput Plateaus
GPU throughput cannot increase indefinitely with batch size. At some batch size
, the compute ceiling is hit and throughput plateaus while latency continues to grow. Identifying
for your model and hardware tells you when to stop increasing
and instead add more GPU replicas:

from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams
import time

MODEL = "meta-llama/Llama-3-8b-hf"
FIXED_OUTPUT_LEN = 128
PROMPT = "Describe the history of computing."

results = []
for num_seqs in [8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256]:
    llm = LLM(
        model=MODEL,
        max_num_seqs=num_seqs,
        gpu_memory_utilization=0.85,
    )
    params = SamplingParams(max_tokens=FIXED_OUTPUT_LEN, temperature=0.0)
    prompts = [PROMPT] * num_seqs  # exactly num_seqs in one batch

    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    outputs = llm.generate(prompts, params)
    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0

    total_tok = sum(len(o.outputs[0].token_ids) for o in outputs)
    tps = total_tok / elapsed
    results.append((num_seqs, tps, elapsed))
    print(f"batch={num_seqs:>4}: {tps:.0f} tok/s  (total {elapsed:.2f}s)")
    del llm

# Throughput plateaus when batch_size > B*; beyond that, increasing batch only adds latency.
# On A100 with Llama-3-8B, B* ≈ 64–128 for output-length 128.

PagedAttention's throughput gains flow from one mechanism: eliminating memory fragmentation expands the achievable batch size, and a larger batch converts idle GPU tensor cores into useful token generation. Combined with continuous batching's work-conserving scheduler, vLLM consistently achieves 2–4× higher output token throughput than static-batching frameworks at the same hardware cost — measured by output tokens per second at any given TTFT latency target.
